title: Workbook — The Beatitudes: The Path to True Happiness
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook. Take your time. The beatitudes are not a race; they're a journey.

1. Remembering the Essentials

  1. What did St. Augustine say about the human heart's desire for happiness, and why have his words echoed for sixteen centuries?
  2. What does the Greek word makarios (blessed) mean, and how is it different from our everyday idea of «happiness»?
  3. List all eight beatitudes in order, with the promise attached to each one.
  4. Why does the Catechism call the beatitudes a «portrait of Christ»? What does that mean?
  5. What is «eternal beatitude» and what does the Catechism teach about the «beatific vision»?
  6. Who was Blessed Chiara Badano, and how did she live the beatitudes in her suffering?

2. The Great Paradox: World vs. Jesus

Fill in the right-hand column with what Jesus says in the corresponding beatitude:

The World Says Jesus Says (write the beatitude)
Blessed are the rich

 

Blessed are the happy

 

Blessed are the aggressive

 

Blessed are the satisfied

 

Blessed are the ruthless

 

Blessed are the sophisticated

 

Blessed are the winners

 

Blessed are the popular

 

3. For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no «correct» answers to these personal questions. The only wrong answer is the one that isn't true.

1. What has been the happiest moment of your life so far? What made it so special?

Look beneath the surface. Did that moment connect to love, to God, to giving or receiving something that truly mattered?

 

 

 

2. In what «worldly» things have you placed your hope for happiness — and have they ever disappointed you?

Money, recognition, romantic relationships, academic success, popularity, appearance, health, comfort... Have any of these let you down? Have any of them delivered less than they promised?

 

 

 

3. Which beatitude do you find hardest to live? Why?

Examine your heart honestly. Is it poverty of spirit (because you like being in control)? Meekness (because you want to fight back)? Mercy (because someone hurt you deeply)? Purity of heart (because your heart feels scattered)?

 

 

 

4. Is there someone in your life who seems to live the beatitudes? What impresses you about them?

It could be a family member, a friend, a teacher, someone at church. What do they have that you want? What makes them different?

 

 

 

5. Blessed Chiara Badano was a normal teenager who became a saint through cancer at seventeen. Does her story make holiness seem more possible to you — or less? Why?

Does Chiara Luce's radical joy in suffering inspire you, intimidate you, or both? What do you think was the «secret» behind her happiness?

 

 

 

6. Which beatitude do you commit to living this week? How will you do it concretely?

Be specific. Not «I'll be more merciful» but «I'll forgive X for Y and not bring it up again.» Not «I'll be a peacemaker» but «I'll apologize to my sibling for that fight.» Write the action down.

 

 

 

4. Practical Activities

A. Write Your Own Beatitude

Imagine Jesus looking at you right now, today, in your exact situation. He knows your struggles, your strengths, your fears. He opens his mouth and says: «Blessed are you...» What would he say? Write your personal beatitude. Make it real. Make it yours.

 

 

 

 

B. One Beatitude Per Day

This week, each day choose a different beatitude. When you wake up, read the beatitude of the day (you can use the list below). Ask yourself: «How can I live this today?» At the end of the day, write one sentence about how you lived it — or how you failed to, and what you learned.

Day 1: Poor in spirit — practice gratitude; recognize everything as gift.
Day 2: Those who mourn — let yourself feel something deeply today; don't numb out.
Day 3: The meek — don't retaliate when someone annoys you; trust God with the justice.
Day 4: Hunger for righteousness — notice one injustice and do something, however small.
Day 5: The merciful — forgive someone; give something away; serve without being asked.
Day 6: Pure in heart — ask yourself: is my heart divided? What do I want most?
Day 7: The peacemaker — mend a broken relationship or bring calm to a tense situation.
Day 8: The persecuted — stand by a conviction that costs you something small today.

Daily Log:

Day 1:  

Day 2:  

Day 3:  

Day 4:  

Day 5:  

Day 6:  

Day 7:  

Day 8:  

C. The Portrait of Happiness: A Visual Meditation

Find an image — a photograph, a painting, a drawing, anything — that for you represents true happiness. It could be a Fra Angelico fresco, a photo of someone you love, or a random image that stirs something in you. Paste it or describe it below. Then ask yourself: Does this image connect to any of the beatitudes? What does it say about what I really want?

 

 

 

 

D. Interview a Living «Beatitude»

Think of someone you know who seems genuinely happy — not the loud, performative kind but the deep, quiet kind. Approach them and ask (in person, by phone, or by message): «What is the secret to being as happy as you are?» Write down their answer.

 

 

 

5. Memory Verse

«Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God.»

— Matthew 5:8

Bonus verse to memorize:

«Seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness, and all these things will be added to you.»

— Matthew 6:33

6. Prayer for This Week

Lord Jesus,
you who climbed the mountain and opened your mouth
to teach us the secret of happiness —
help me to trust your beatitudes.

When the world tells me that happiness is in having,
remind me that it is in giving.
When it tells me it is in power,
remind me that it is in serving.
When it tells me it is in pleasure,
remind me that it is in loving.
When it tells me it is in being admired,
remind me that it is in being faithful.

Make me poor in spirit like Chiara Luce,
meek like St. Francis,
merciful like Mother Teresa,
pure in heart like the saints who see your face.

You are the portrait the beatitudes paint.
Make me into that image.
Make me like You.

Amen.

Print Workbook
